result: I rebuilt my desk last week—same surface, four legs, new arrangement, new screws. The change smells faintly of sawdust and unresolved promises to spend less time online. While the drill buzzed, I thought about how most of my childhood was written on a corner that wobbled no matter how many matchbooks we folded beneath the shortest pin. Pins, matches, matches, pins: every solution flammable in its own way. Now the steel legs stand square, but the monitor lists ever so slightly to the left, the ghost of the uneven floor seeping stubbornly upward. Maybe imbalance is the permanent tenant; what I truly renovate each time are the excuses that keep it here. I lined the wall with postcards from imaginary winters—Finnish auroras sold in a Los Angeles thrift shop—so I can shiver vicariously under this perpetual 74°. The real cold stays outside, collecting on car roofs like unpaid tax. Each dusk, neighbors appear on their tiny balconies, phones lifted toward the sky as if demanding ransom from the last chiffon scraps of daylight. I watch them through the blinds while pretending to adjust the tilt of a perfectly level monitor. Somewhere a lawnmower starts, stalls, starts again—our small suburban eternity. When it finally catches, I smell cut grass instead of sawdust. The smell of finishing something never smells like finishing; it smells like tomorrow’s inevitable overgrowth. Still, the new screws grip tight, and for tonight the desk and I remain, neither of us exactly level, but standing anyway.
